process_context.operating_conditions: atmospheric to low-pressure distillation of crude fractions; elevated temperature (≈150–300°C)

failure_mechanism.root_cause: Inadequate mechanical integrity management leading to undetected corrosion-thinning and subsequent catastrophic rupture of a hydrocarbon overhead line.
failure_mechanism.causal_chain: Poor inspection program → undetected wall thinning due to acidic corrosion → loss of containment → hydrocarbon vapor release → ignition → explosion
failure_mechanism.physical_principles: Corrosion fatigue, stress corrosion cracking, flammability limits, deflagration-to-detonation transition

protection_system.safeguard_failures: Relief valves were not sized for the failure mode; PHA had not identified the specific corrosion mechanism in overhead piping; fire detection lacked coverage for vapor cloud ignition initiation.
csb_recommendations: Implement risk-based inspection (RBI) programs focused on corrosion mechanisms in overhead systems
Require revalidation of PHA for all piping systems exposed to wet acidic vapors
Mandate dispersion modeling and ignition source control for atmospheric hydrocarbon releases

embedding_text: The Silver Eagle explosion involved a corroded overhead hydrocarbon line in an atmospheric distillation unit. Light naphtha vapors leaked due to loss of containment (HAZOP deviation: No containment), caused by undetected acid corrosion from wet H2S/CO2 service. The released flammable vapor formed a cloud that ignited, causing a large-scale vapor cloud explosion near residential areas. Root cause was deficient mechanical integrity practices and PHA gaps.
